head: 44c1476
summary: PR #388 introduces a clean, well-scoped MatcherConfig abstraction and a small matchers package. The core API (MatcherConfig + matchers.Match) is appropriate, and PolicyWithMatcher is a justified wrapper because the per-matcher Empty semantics genuinely cannot be derived from CachePolicy alone. The bulk of maintainability risk is around residual legacy compatibility surface, duplicated matching logic in cache_policy.go for GETs, a few merge artifacts, an unused MatchResult type, fragile structural detection of auto-generated defaults in validation, and a real but undocumented behavior change where the legacy CachePolicyConfig.Finality zero value now narrows matches to Unknown. The new system is sound; the cleanup needed is concrete and bounded.

findings.title: Dead code: MatcherConfig MatchResult struct never referenced
findings.evidence: type MatchResult struct { Matched bool; Action MatcherAction } -- ripgrep across the repo finds zero references outside this declaration.
findings.why_it_matters: Adds a public type to the common package that suggests an API contract that does not exist. Every future reader will wonder whether this is meant to be returned by Match() or by validateMatchers(). It will rot.
findings.recommendation: Delete the type. If there is a future use for a tuple result, reintroduce it then with a real consumer.

findings.title: Skipped 150-line legacy test marked as a pre-merge TODO
findings.evidence: TestSetDefaults_NetworkConfig_FailsafeMatchMethod is wrapped in t.Skip("Behavior removed by PR #388 (matchers); see review comments #11/#12"). The header comment explicitly says "they should be replaced with new coverage of the matchers-based defaulting logic before the PR merges" -- that pre-merge TODO is unresolved.
findings.why_it_matters: Skipped tests with long bodies are 100% pure liability: they are never green, never red, never maintained, and a future developer cannot tell whether they describe current behavior or removed behavior. The author explicitly recognized this.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) delete the entire function now and rely on git history for the prior behavior contract, or (b) port each subtest to the matchers-based path before merging. Do not merge with the t.Skip in place.

findings.title: Merge-resolution artifact left in inline comment
findings.evidence: // Validate the cached result's age against the policy's TTL (from main) -- the trailing "(from main)" parenthetical is a leftover marker from the manual conflict resolution called out in the merge commit 44c1476.
findings.why_it_matters: Makes the diff look unfinished and confuses any future reader who searches for source attribution markers.
findings.recommendation: Strip the "(from main)" suffix. Audit the merge commit's other 4 conflict resolutions for similar markers (the next line at 267 has "// Remember which policy actually returned data" which is fine).

findings.title: Documentation typo: stray closing paren in matcher.mdx link
findings.evidence: - [failsafe policies](/config/failsafe)) -- two closing parens, breaks the markdown link rendering.
findings.why_it_matters: Tiny, but the matcher docs are the authoritative reference being introduced by this PR; a broken link in the first list of bullets undermines confidence.

findings.title: PR-number and review-comment citations baked into source comments
findings.evidence: Multiple inline comments reference "matchers PR (#388)" and "review comments #11/#12 for context". Once the PR is merged, the PR number is implicit in git blame and the review-comment numbers point nowhere.
findings.why_it_matters: Comments that depend on transient PR-review URLs become stale immediately on merge. They also hint that the rationale lives elsewhere (in a PR conversation that future readers cannot reach).
findings.recommendation: Rewrite each comment to describe the rule itself, not the source of the rule. Example: replace "matchers PR #388 deliberately replaced per-method/finality merging with 'user policies override defaults entirely'" with "User-defined failsafe policies override defaults entirely; do not merge defaults in by method/finality matching."

findings.title: Naming asymmetry between FailsafeConfig legacy (MatchMethod/MatchFinality) and CachePolicy/MatcherConfig (Method/Finality)
findings.evidence: MatcherConfig fields are Method/Finality. CachePolicyConfig deprecated fields are Method/Finality. FailsafeConfig deprecated fields are MatchMethod/MatchFinality with a Match prefix. So a failsafe user migrating must drop the prefix; a cache user must not. Nothing in the deprecation comment (`// Deprecated: Use Matchers instead`) explains this rename.
findings.why_it_matters: Migration path is harder than necessary. Anyone copy-pasting a failsafe matchMethod into a matcher will get YAML decode errors with KnownFields(true). A one-line note in the Deprecated comment would prevent the support tickets.
findings.recommendation: Update the FailsafeConfig deprecation comment to read `// Deprecated: Use Matchers instead. Note: matcher field is named 'method' (no 'match' prefix).` Same for matchFinality. Mirror in docs/pages/config/failsafe.mdx migration section.

findings.title: MatchesForGetWithMatcher reimplements per-field matching that lives in matchers.MatchConfig
findings.evidence: MatchesForGetWithMatcher inlines its own network/method/params matching loop (lines 142-164) instead of calling matchers.MatchConfig. Only the finality branch differs (GET wants 'finalized requests can also match unfinalized policies'); everything above it is a copy. matchers.MatchParams is invoked but not the surrounding wrapper.
findings.why_it_matters: If MatchConfig ever changes (new field type, new wildcard semantics, new escape rules), the GET path silently diverges. Cache reads and writes would obey different matching semantics for the same matcher config. This is the highest-impact maintainability hazard in the diff.
findings.recommendation: Factor out per-field helpers in matchers package (matchNetwork, matchMethod, matchParamsField, matchFinality) and have both MatchConfig and the GET path compose them. Or add a `looseFinalityForGet bool` option to MatchConfig and call it from both.

findings.title: MatchesForSet/MatchesForGet are thin (bool, error) wrappers used only by tests
findings.evidence: Both functions just call the WithMatcher variant and discard the matcher. Production callers (architecture/evm/json_rpc_cache.go::findSetPolicies, findGetPolicies) all use the WithMatcher variants. Grep shows the non-WithMatcher signatures appear only in evm_json_rpc_cache_test.go and data/cache_policy_test.go.
findings.why_it_matters: Two surface APIs for the same operation, where one is strictly weaker (drops the matcher) and pretends to return an error that is always nil. Future readers will guess wrong about which is the intended public API.
findings.recommendation: Delete MatchesForSet and MatchesForGet, update tests to call MatchesForSetWithMatcher/MatchesForGetWithMatcher and ignore the second return value. Optionally rename the WithMatcher variants to just MatchesForSet/MatchesForGet now that there is one form.

findings.title: validateMatchers detects auto-generated default by structural pattern-matching the field values
findings.evidence: isAutoGeneratedDefault := matcher.Method == "*" && matcher.Network == "" && len(matcher.Params) == 0 && len(matcher.Finality) == 0 && matcher.Action == MatcherInclude. This is a structural fingerprint of the matcher that convertLegacyMatchers in defaults.go synthesizes. There is no shared constant or marker.
findings.why_it_matters: Two files (defaults.go and validation.go) silently agree on the exact shape of a synthetic matcher. If convertLegacyMatchers ever adds a field (e.g., setting Action to MatcherInclude only when something), validation will silently classify it as user-provided and inject an unwanted catch-all exclude in front of it -- a quiet behavior break.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) add an unexported `autoGenerated bool` flag on MatcherConfig (and skip it in the include/exclude prefix logic), or (b) move the catch-all-default decision into validateMatchers itself so the two halves are colocated, or (c) add a shared helper `IsCatchAllInclude(*MatcherConfig) bool` consumed by both sides.

findings.title: convertLegacyMatchers narrows behavior of legacy CachePolicyConfig.Finality (zero value now means 'Unknown only')
findings.evidence: matcher.Finality = []DataFinalityState{c.Finality} -- always emitted, even when the user did not set finality. Once converted, MatchConfig only treats finality as wildcard when the slice is empty, so a single-element slice with the zero value (DataFinalityStateUnknown) restricts matches to Unknown only.
findings.why_it_matters: Existing YAML configs that omit `finality:` on a cache policy used to behave as 'match any finality' (Validate did not require it; the old engine treated unset as wildcard). After this PR they silently match only Unknown-finality requests, which means most production traffic stops being cached. There is no warning, no log, no validation error.
findings.recommendation: Distinguish unset from zero. Easiest path: change CachePolicyConfig.Finality to a *DataFinalityState pointer (matching the existing convention for *bool/*int defaults), or only emit matcher.Finality when c.Finality != DataFinalityStateUnknown. Add a dedicated test asserting that a legacy policy without `finality:` matches finalized requests.

findings.title: Asymmetric legacy-fallback paths between Network.getFailsafeExecutor and Upstream.getFailsafeExecutor
findings.evidence: networks.go falls through to a method/finality wildcard match for executors with config==nil (and the legacy block ends up returning the synthetic default). upstream.go skips the entire block when fe.config==nil and falls back to `failsafeExecutors[len-1]`. Both reach the same synthetic default but via different code paths, with the legacy block in upstream.go being functionally unreachable (every fsCfg has at least one Matcher after Normalize).
findings.why_it_matters: Two near-identical selection algorithms in two packages, drifting in subtle ways. Anyone fixing a selection bug in one will likely miss the other. The legacy block in upstream.go also gives a misleading impression that legacy fallback still does work.
findings.recommendation: Extract `matchers.SelectFailsafeExecutor(ctx, executors, req)` (or similar) to the matchers package, or at least move the loop into a shared helper in upstream/. Delete the dead legacy block in upstream.go now that all fsCfg go through Normalize.

findings.title: NewCachePolicy mutates its input config via SetDefaults
findings.evidence: Constructor calls cfg.SetDefaults() to ensure Matchers is populated, with a comment noting idempotency. This means NewCachePolicy is no longer a pure read of the config -- it writes back to the caller's struct.
findings.why_it_matters: The framework already calls Config.SetDefaults at load time; calling it again from a constructor is defensive duplication. It also breaks the invariant that constructors do not mutate inputs, which makes it harder to reason about reuse of the same *CachePolicyConfig in multiple places.
findings.recommendation: Trust the load-time SetDefaults pass and have NewCachePolicy assert `len(cfg.Matchers) > 0` instead of fixing it. Tests that construct a config inline can call SetDefaults explicitly. If you keep the call, add an assertion comment that this is the only mutation point so reviewers stop asking.

findings.title: Trailing fallback `failsafeExecutors[len-1]` relies on unstated ordering invariant
findings.evidence: Both selectors end with `if len(executors) > 0 { return executors[len-1] }`. The synthetic default executor is always appended last in NewNetwork/NewUpstream, so this works. But the invariant is implicit, untested, and one reordering line away from a silent bug (some other config-driven executor would be returned as the default).
findings.why_it_matters: Selection of the default executor is a load-bearing path; today it is correct by convention only. A regression here would manifest as 'wrong timeout/retry' bugs that are very hard to attribute.
findings.recommendation: Either give the default executor an explicit field (e.g., `isDefault bool` or store it as a separate `n.defaultExecutor`) and select it by that, or filter `for _, fe := range executors { if fe.config == nil { return fe } }`. Add a unit test that asserts `executors[len-1].config == nil`.

findings.title: Trivial dead branch in matchers.Match networkId extraction
findings.evidence: networkId := ""; if req.NetworkId() != "" { networkId = req.NetworkId() } -- this is exactly equivalent to networkId := req.NetworkId(), with one extra method call.
findings.why_it_matters: Microscopic, but the pattern suggests there was once a reason for the guard (perhaps an older NetworkId() that returned a sentinel). Future readers will pause to figure out why.

findings.title: No warning when both legacy and new matcher fields are set on the same config
findings.evidence: convertLegacyMatchers short-circuits with `if len(c.Matchers) > 0 { return }`. A user who mixes legacy and new fields gets the new ones honored and the legacy ones silently dropped, with no log line or validation error.
findings.why_it_matters: Mixed configs are easy to produce while migrating (e.g., a snippet copied from old docs alongside a new matchers block). Silent precedence is a debugging time-sink. A warning log is cheap insurance during the deprecation window.
findings.recommendation: When `len(c.Matchers) > 0` and any legacy field is also set, log a one-time WARN per config explaining that the legacy fields are ignored. Keep the precedence semantics as-is.

positive_findings.title: PolicyWithMatcher is a justified abstraction, not premature
positive_findings.rationale: The reviewer prompt asked specifically whether PolicyWithMatcher is a useful wrapper. It is: a single CachePolicy can have multiple Matchers, each with its own `empty` semantics. Without binding the matched matcher to the policy at lookup time, the cache layer would have to recompute which matcher matched on every Set/Get. Two callers (json_rpc_cache.go::Get at line 338 and shouldCacheResponse at line 900) already use the matcher-specific EmptyState. The abstraction earns its keep.

positive_findings.title: matchers.Match has a small, focused public surface
positive_findings.rationale: Three exported functions (Match, MatchConfig, MatchParams) plus two private helpers, totaling 185 lines. Match takes (ctx, configs, req, resp) -- the right signature for the failsafe selection callers. MatchConfig is the testable primitive used by cache_policy.go. The split is appropriate; the API does not leak too many abstractions.

residual_risks.detail: Both CachePolicyConfig (Network/Method/Params/Finality/Empty) and FailsafeConfig (MatchMethod/MatchFinality) carry `// Deprecated: Use Matchers instead` comments. There is no removal version, no deprecation log on use, and no docs section explaining when these fields will be deleted. Code in `convertLegacyMatchers` will need to live on for at least one release; without a target version it tends to live forever.
residual_risks.mitigation: Pick a target removal version (e.g., v1.x), document it in CHANGELOG and the deprecation comments, and add a startup-time WARN log when any legacy field is present.

residual_risks.risk: M10's silent finality narrowing could regress production caching
residual_risks.detail: Existing users of `cache.policies[*]` configs that omit `finality:` will see their cache hit rate drop sharply after upgrade because the synthesized matcher now restricts to DataFinalityStateUnknown. This is not detectable by validation and not covered by tests in the diff.
residual_risks.mitigation: See M10 recommendation. At minimum, add a release-notes call-out and a startup-time scan that warns when a legacy CachePolicyConfig has `finality` unset.

residual_risks.risk: Selection algorithm divergence between Network and Upstream
residual_risks.detail: Two parallel implementations of getFailsafeExecutor that happen to agree today. Future bug fixes will trend toward fixing one and forgetting the other.
residual_risks.mitigation: See M11 recommendation: extract a single shared selector.

testing_gaps.gap: No test asserts that a legacy CachePolicyConfig with omitted `finality:` still matches finalized requests
testing_gaps.detail: Directly tied to M10. The new defaults_test.go coverage focuses on FailsafeConfig conversion. CachePolicyConfig legacy conversion is tested only with explicitly-set finality.
testing_gaps.suggested_test: TestCachePolicyConfig_LegacyFinalityOmittedMatchesAllFinalities -- construct CachePolicyConfig{Method: "eth_call"}, call SetDefaults, and assert MatchesForGetWithMatcher returns true for both Finalized and Realtime requests.

testing_gaps.gap: No test asserts default-executor invariant in failsafeExecutors
testing_gaps.detail: Both Network.getFailsafeExecutor and Upstream.getFailsafeExecutor depend on the synthetic default being last in the slice. No unit test pins this.
testing_gaps.suggested_test: TestNewNetwork_AppendsDefaultExecutorLast -- assert `executors[len-1].config == nil`. Same for upstream.

testing_gaps.gap: No test mixes legacy and new matcher fields in the same config
testing_gaps.detail: Tied to M15. Behavior of a config that sets both `matchMethod:` and `matchers:` is unspecified by tests, even though it is the most likely shape during user migration.
testing_gaps.suggested_test: TestFailsafeConfig_MixedLegacyAndMatchers -- assert that Matchers wins and that the legacy fields do not corrupt or override the matcher list.

testing_gaps.detail: The deleted defaulting algorithm was tested by ~150 lines; the new 'user-policies-stand-alone' rule has no equivalent dedicated test. The rule is implicit in defaults_test.go scattered cases.
testing_gaps.suggested_test: TestNetworkConfig_UserFailsafeOverridesAllDefaults -- given network defaults with two failsafe configs and a user-defined failsafe, assert defaults are entirely ignored and only the user policies remain after SetDefaults.
